item.arguments: Use $research to investigate how frontier models should do high-quality topic research before adding a research helper skill to an engineering workflow plugin.
success_definition: Frames the research topic clearly.
Chooses an explicit research execution mode with a rationale.
Breaks the topic into concrete research threads.
Ranks findings instead of dumping links.
Keeps facts, inferences, and open questions distinct.
Ends with a recommendation.
Returns a compact in-turn brief by default instead of implying eager persistence.

item.arguments: Use $research to compare current best practices for a new researcher agent across orchestration, retrieval, and evaluation, then save a reusable brief for later product shaping.
success_definition: Chooses orchestrated research with a bounded rationale rather than treating all research as single-threaded.
Breaks the topic into independent threads.
Keeps source references traceable.
Ends with a recommendation.
Returns reusable save guidance or a `docs/research/` path for later shaping work.

item.arguments: Use $research on this repo to understand whether shaping stages should proactively trigger research or keep it user-invoked only.
success_definition: Grounds the answer in local repo truth.
Uses external context as secondary evidence rather than replacing repo truth.
Explains the activation tradeoff clearly.
Ends with a recommendation the next shaping stage could use.
Keeps the result compact and reusable without assuming a saved brief.

item.arguments: Use $research to investigate local workflow and naming cleanup opportunities in this repo. No external research.
success_definition: Honors the no-external-research constraint.
Chooses inline research instead of escalating complexity.
Uses local repo evidence only.
Still ranks the findings and keeps the brief structured.
Ends with a recommendation.
Does not imply published-source browsing happened or that a saved brief was required.
